preFix.title: Running a new copper pipe through a wall
preFix.subtitle: Drill and sleeve a masonry wall and make soldered copper joints
preFix.excerpt: How to run a 15 mm copper pipe through a masonry or timber-frame wall: drilling the correct-sized hole, fitting a plastic sleeve, making flux-and-solder capillary joints on each side, and supporting the pipe run correctly.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. BS 6700 Water Supply in Buildings.

content.text: Running copper pipe through a solid wall needs three things: the right-sized hole,
to protect the copper inside the wall, and sound
joints on each side. The soldering step looks difficult but follows a clear sequence once the surface is prepared.

15 mm copper pipe and capillary elbows or straight connectors as required. 22 mm plastic conduit for sleeving (25 mm for a snug fit around 15 mm copper). MAPP gas blowtorch, lead-free solder, water-soluble flux, and emery cloth. 15 mm pipe cutter. SDS drill with a 22 to 25 mm core bit or masonry drill bit long enough to go through the wall thickness.
Step 1, drill the wall and fit the sleeve

Mark the hole position, checking the route is clear of other pipes and cables using a detector. Drill from one face through with the SDS drill; if the wall is thick, drill from both sides to meet in the middle. Cut the plastic conduit to the exact wall thickness and press it into the hole; it should be a snug fit, held in place by friction and later by filler around the outside faces.
Step 2, prepare the copper ends for soldering

Cut each pipe end square with the pipe cutter and remove the internal burr by rotating the cutter's reamer. Rub the outside of each pipe end and the inside of each capillary fitting with emery cloth until bright copper shows. Apply water-soluble flux to both surfaces immediately using a small brush. Push the fitting fully onto the pipe; a small bead of flux at the mouth of the fitting confirms correct depth.

Hold the blowtorch flame at the middle of the fitting, not at the pipe. Move the flame slowly around the fitting for 10 to 15 seconds until the flux starts to bubble and brown. Touch the end of the solder wire to the gap between the pipe and fitting at the mouth: if the temperature is correct, the solder is drawn in immediately and a bright ring appears around the joint. Remove the flame and allow the joint to cool for 30 seconds before moving the pipe. Wipe the hot joint with a damp cloth to remove flux residue.

Soldering with water in the pipe. Drain the section fully before soldering; a trickle of water at the joint prevents the copper reaching soldering temperature and the solder balls up rather than flowing in.

Heating the pipe instead of the fitting. The fitting is the thicker piece and needs more heat. If the pipe gets hotter than the fitting, solder flows along the pipe away from the joint rather than into it.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
